conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have one child, Alex, who's 17 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in education or training anymore. He finished school and didn't continue with any courses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to help determine your eligibility.

Is Alex under 16 years old, or has he turned 16 yet?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 17, so yes, he's already turned 16.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Just to confirm I have the complete picture:

Are you responsible for Alex and does he live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. I live in the UK and Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Let me ask one more important question:

Do you have the main responsibility for Alex? This means you're responsible for their day-to-day care and upbringing, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have the main responsibility for Alex. I take care of him day-to-day, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: Child Benefit can be claimed for children under 16, or under 20 if they continue in approved full-time non-advanced education or training. Since Alex is 17 and is no longer in education or training, he doesn't meet the eligibility requirements.

If Alex's circumstances change and he decides to return to full-time education or approved training in the future, you may become eligible to claim Child Benefit for him again (until he turns 20).

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: According to UK Child Benefit eligibility rules, you can claim Child Benefit for a child under 16, or under 20 if they stay in approved education or training. Alex is 17 years old and is not in full-time non-advanced education or approved training. Since Alex has left school and is not continuing with any courses or training, and is over 16, he does not meet the age and education requirements for Child Benefit eligibility.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has main responsibility for their 17-year-old child Alex who lives with them in the UK. However, Alex is not eligible for Child Benefit because he is over 16 and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit eligibility extends beyond age 16 only if the young person remains in full-time non-advanced education or approved training until they turn 20.
